data.title: SoftBank Corp. Draws 4.2 Billion Demand For Debut Euro Bonds Amid AI Concerns
data.description:
SoftBank Corp. (SOBKY) is tapping the euro bond market for the first time, and early momentum suggests investors are engaging, though with a measured focus on risk tied to its parent. The telecom unit of SoftBank Group Corp. is offering euro-denominated benchmark bonds across six- and 10-year maturities, drawing more than 4.2 billion in demand across the tranches. On the back of that demand, pricing was tightened by around 30 basis points, with the 500 million June 2032 notes indicated near 105 basis points above mid-swaps and the longer-dated June 2036 portion around 140 basis points.

The credit profile continues to be shaped by developments at the parent level. While SoftBank Corp.'s position within Japan's concentrated telecom market could provide underlying support, investor attention remains centered on the broader capital allocation strategy at SoftBank Group. S&P Global Ratings recently revised its outlook on the parent to negative, citing increased exposure to artificial intelligence investments, including planned financing linked to OpenAI. At the same time, SoftBank Group is exploring raising as much as $40 billion in the loan market, which could represent its largest-ever dollar borrowing and adds to the discussion around funding pressures.

Market indicators appear to reflect this evolving risk backdrop. SoftBank Group's shares are down about 14% this year, underperforming the broader market, while credit default swaps have widened roughly 20%, suggesting a reassessment of credit risk. The group has also turned to hybrid issuance, pricing 418 billion ($2.6 billion) of such bonds at a 4.97% initial five-year coupon, the highest level for this type of corporate debt. Even so, rating agencies continue to position SoftBank Corp. above its parent, and some analysts suggest a downgrade could be unlikely, at least while an OpenAI IPO remains a fairly near-term possibility.

data.title: Airbus Q1 Deliveries Fall 16% To 114 Jets, Lowest Since 2009
data.description:
Airbus SE (EADSY) is entering 2026 with a slower start than expected, after first-quarter deliveries came in at their lowest level since 2009, a development that could raise questions about how achievable its full-year targets may be under current conditions. The company delivered 114 commercial aircraft in the first three months of the year, down 16% from the same period last year, as ongoing engine shortages and manufacturing issues continued to weigh on output.

Management had previously guided for around 870 aircraft deliveries in 2026, though that outlook was set before the escalation of the Iran war, which is now adding another layer of pressure across the aviation sector. Airlines are facing higher jet fuel costs and, in some cases, curbing operations amid the conflict, which could influence demand patterns. Monthly delivery trends highlight the uneven production cadence, with just 19 aircraft delivered in January, followed by 35 in February and 60 in March, suggesting some recovery but not yet a steady ramp.

To reach its annual goal, Airbus would likely need a meaningful acceleration in deliveries over the remaining nine months, implying a heavily back-end loaded schedule that analysts suggest remains possible at this stage, though not without execution risk. At the same time, Boeing Co. has yet to report quarterly figures, but has recently outpaced Airbus in monthly shipments and regained the annual sales lead in 2025, while Airbus continues to navigate prior production disruptions, including A320 fuselage issues that affected last year's delivery plans.

data.title: President Trump Just Delivers Blunt Message to Palantir Investors
data.description:
Palantir Technologies shares are in focus on Friday after President Donald Trump praised the company's defense technology in a social media post.

Trump wrote that Palantir "has proven to have great war fighting capabilities and equipment" and added, "Just ask our enemies!!!" The stock, down as much as 6% earlier, trimmed its decline to about 2% following the remarks.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives kept an Outperform rating and $230 price target on Palantir, pushing back against recent competitive concerns. Ives noted that Palantir's U.S. commercial revenue expanded 137% from a year ago while U.S. government business accelerated 66%.

The analyst acknowledged that Anthropic's new multi-agent product may create broader software sector headwinds but said it does not appear to be eating into Palantir's growth trajectory. Palantir had dropped more than 15% in the five sessions leading into Friday amid unease over the Anthropic launch.

Ives expects Palantir's momentum in both commercial and public sector accounts to continue.

data.title: Commodities Surge 35% As BofA Flags Shift From Stocks In Anything But Bonds Trade
data.description:
The tone in markets may be starting to tilt. Bank of America strategist Michael Hartnett suggests commodities could be positioning for a stronger run into the back half of the decade, as investors continue to lean into the anything but bonds trade. While equities have led flows in recent years, Hartnett indicates that leadership could rotate, with commodities potentially emerging as the preferred hedge against inflation pressures, geopolitical uncertainty, and a softer dollar environment.

Hartnett points to a backdrop shaped by fiscal excess and persistent macro imbalances, which could limit the scope for a sustained bull market in government bonds. Instead, bond markets may experience intermittent rallies rather than a durable uptrend. At the same time, geopolitical developmentsfrom tensions in the Middle East to the intensifying artificial intelligence raceare reinforcing the importance of supply chains and access to critical resources. Governments are increasingly focused on securing energy supplies and key inputs such as rare earth minerals, which remain essential for both industrial output and advanced technology manufacturing.

Recent market performance may already reflect this shift in positioning. The Bloomberg Commodity Index has climbed 35% since the start of 2025, more than doubling the return of the S&P 500 over the same period, while Treasuries have gained less than 7%. Oil prices have risen sharply amid supply disruptions linked to geopolitical tensions, while metals including gold, silver, and copper have been supported by central bank demand and the expansion of AI infrastructure. Hartnett adds that commodities could remain central to the investment narrative, with broader positioning also favoring international and small-cap equities, alongside near-term trades such as yield curve steepeners, Chinese technology exposure, and selective allocations to consumer and semiconductor names tied to ongoing AI spending.

data.title: Mina Protocol Published Mesa Progress Report for March 2026
data.description:
Mina Protocol published its Mesa progress report for March 2026 on April 10th. The report covers testnet validation, infrastructure work, and the next steps for Mesa.

Refer to the official tweet by MINA:

Mina Protocol (httpz) 🪶
@MinaProtocol

🗓ï¸Mesa progress: March edition

Find the latest progress update covering testnet validation, infrastructure work, and next steps here: https://t.co/BtRjmOW2vb pic.twitter.com/enqHzCUgRe

Apr 10, 2026

MINA Info

Mina Protocol is a Layer 1 protocol designed to provide scalability and privacy in a distributed network. The protocol utilizes zk-SNARKs (zero-knowledge succinct non-interactive arguments of knowledge) technology, which allows it to be called the lightest cryptocurrency protocol with a compressed blockchain (22 KB). This constant value makes it incredibly compact compared to other blockchains.

Instead of storing the full blockchain data, participants in the Mina Protocol network only store transaction validation proofs. This enables any device, even mobile ones, to become a full node in the Mina network, significantly reducing the entry barrier and increasing decentralization.

The Mina network is secured by proof-of-stake (“PoS”) consensus called Ouroboros Samisika.

Based on Cardano’s Ouroboros, Ouroboros Samisika is a PoS consensus mechanism that requires far less computing power than Bitcoin’s proof-of-work (“PoW”) protocol.

data.title: BNB to Host Meetup in Hong Kong on April 19th
data.description:
BNB will host the BNB HK Super Meetup in Hong Kong on April 19th. The event is aimed at builders, founders, and the broader BNB ecosystem community.

Refer to the official tweet by BNB:

BNB Chain
@BNBCHAIN

We are so back! 🇭🇰

Join us at BNB HK Super Meetup on April 19.

A gathering of builders, founders and the community pushing the frontier of the BNB ecosystem.

Special thanks to our sponsors: @predictdotfun, @alibaba_cloud, @OndoFinance, @StableStock, @Hertzflow_xyz,â€¦ pic.twitter.com/JXHpblsNKI

Apr 10, 2026

BNB Info

BNB is the native coin that powers the BNB Chain ecosystem, including the BNB Beacon Chain and BNB Smart Chain. It serves several functions, such as reducing trading fees on the Binance exchange, paying for transaction fees on both the BNB Beacon Chain and BNB Smart Chain, and being utilized in games and DApps within the BNB Chain ecosystem. It’s also employed to participate in token sales hosted on the Binance Launchpad and provide liquidity on Binance Liquid Swap. Furthermore, BNB can be used for real-world applications, including online and in-store purchases and travel bookings on platforms like Travala.com.

As of September 2020, with the launch of the BNB Smart Chain, BNB can exist in three forms: BNB BEP-2 on the BNB Beacon Chain, BNB BEP-20 on the BNB Smart Chain, and BNB ERC-20 on the Ethereum network.

data.title: Glencore Accepts 62 Cent Power Deal As Smelter Restart Awaits Approval
data.description:
Glencore (GLEN) is edging closer to a potential restart of its South African ferrochrome operations after its joint venture with Merafe Resources provisionally accepted a lower electricity tariff, a development that could ease one of the sector's most persistent cost pressures. The venture has been in discussions with Eskom Holdings over a power supply agreement priced at 62 cents ($0.03) per kilowatt hour, with revised terms and conditions now communicated by Eskom's board and conditionally agreed to, according to the company's statement.

Management described the progress as significant and positive, though final acceptance still depends on approvals from Samancor Chrome and the National Electricity Regulator of South Africa. Eskom indicated the proposed structure could serve as a medium-term arrangement of up to five years, allowing for pricing and contractual flexibility tailored to the commercial realities of individual smelters. That flexibility could be an important lever as operators assess whether previously idled capacity can return to production under more sustainable cost conditions.

The backdrop remains challenging, with South Africa retaining its position as the world's largest producer of chrome ore while its ferrochrome processing industry has been under pressure from rising electricity costs and competition from China. Four of the five smelting complexes within the Glencore-led venture are currently not operational, underscoring the scale of disruption. The joint venture has urged regulators to treat the approval process as urgent and complete it within 30 days, while extending a deadline for potential job cuts to May 11 as negotiations continue.

data.title: Inflation Soared to 3.3% in March, Driven by Higher Gasoline Costs — WSJ
data.description:
By Konrad Putzier

Consumer prices soared in March, pushed higher by skyrocketing gasoline prices.

The numbers

Consumer prices were up 3.3% in March from a year earlier, the Labor Department said Friday, much hotter than February's gain of 2.4%.

It was the highest reading in two years. But it was also in line with the expectations of economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal.

Prices excluding food and energy categories — the so-called core measure economists watch in an effort to better capture inflation's underlying trend — rose 2.6%, slightly below forecasts for a 2.7% increase.

Gasoline prices

Energy prices jumped by 12.5% from a year earlier, a dramatic acceleration from 0.5% in February. Gasoline prices jumped 18.9% and fuel oil surged by 44.2%.

The cost of transportation services, which is affected by fuel costs, rose 4.1% from a year earlier in March, a much faster increase than in February.

Friday's report offers the first snapshot of how the Iran war affected U.S. inflation. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz snarled shipping and sent the price of crude oil and gasoline surging last month.

How the markets reacted

U.S. stock indexes were mixed in midday trading. The Dow Jones Industrial Average was in the red. The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq composite rose. Treasury yields rose slightly.

Cars, food and airline tickets

Used-car prices fell by 3.2% from a year earlier, and the cost of groceries increased at a slower annual pace than in February.

Annual shelter inflation remained unchanged at 3.0%. Apparel prices, which are sensitive to tariffs, rose by 3.4% in March from a year earlier, a faster pace than February's increase of 2.5%. Airline fares surged by 14.9% year over year.

No all-clear

Overall inflation numbers were "better than feared" in March, said Tom Porcelli, chief economist at Wells Fargo, pointing to benign services inflation after excluding energy. Now, much depends on how long the oil shock lasts.

"I don't think anyone should be sounding the all clear after this report, " he said.

What the White House says

In a post on X, White House spokesman Kush Desai acknowledged "short-term disruptions" caused by the war, but touted falling prices for goods like eggs and dairy.

The next few months

If energy prices remain high, that could filter through to higher prices for food — fertilizer is made from natural gas — and other goods.

Oil is used to make everything from lipstick to golf balls, which could eventually make them more expensive. Higher transportation costs could push up the price of food, clothing and other essentials. "We won't feel the bulk of that for perhaps a month or two," said Lindsey Piegza, chief economist at Stifel.

Prices for food will take three to six months to respond to the surge in energy prices, said Samuel Tombs at Pantheon Macroeconomics.

A bite out of spending power

Surging prices ate into household finances. Average weekly earnings fell by 0.9% in March from February after adjusting for inflation, and were up just 0.2% from a year earlier. That drop in real-wage growth hurts the economy because it leaves consumers with less money to spend, weakening demand for goods and services, said Ian Wyatt, chief economist at Huntington Bank.

Still, relatively mild food and rent inflation offered some good news for low-income households, who have been squeezed by high inflation over the past year, Wyatt said.

The mood

Consumers became gloomier in recent weeks. The University of Michigan's consumer sentiment index, also released Friday, fell to its lowest level on record. The initial April reading, of 47.6, was down almost 11% from the month before and well below last April, when the country was gripped by the Trump administration's new tariff announcements.

Quick to rise, slow to fall

A cease-fire in the Iran war was announced this week, but it remains fragile. And economists caution that energy and goods prices that surged after the war started won't immediately fall back to their old levels if the Strait of Hormuz were to fully reopen. Companies are often quick to raise prices and much slower to lower them.

Surveys from the Institute for Supply Management published earlier this week showed more companies paying higher prices to their suppliers. ISM's services prices index in March rose by 7.7 percentage points to 70.7 — the highest level since October 2022. That is a worrying sign because companies may eventually pass some or all of those costs on to their customers, leading to higher inflation.

The Fed's dilemma

Above-target inflation and a cooling labor market have put the Federal Reserve in a tight spot. After several years of shocks that have made businesses and consumers more accustomed to price increases, officials worry that lowering rates too aggressively to shore up the job market could risk sustaining higher inflation.

Write to Konrad Putzier at konrad.putzier@wsj.com

data.title: Gas Prices Jump To $4.166 Drive Surge In US Pawn Loan Demand
data.description:
Rising fuel costs are starting to show up in real-time consumer behavior, with pawn shop operators across the US reporting a noticeable increase in loan demand over the past month. The move in gasoline pricesfrom $2.984 per gallon before the Iran war to $4.166 as of April 8appears to be tightening household budgets, particularly for lower-income consumers who tend to spend a larger share of income on fuel. Data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics also showed US inflation accelerating in March at the fastest pace in nearly four years, largely driven by higher gas prices, reinforcing what operators are seeing on the ground.

That pressure may not be limited to lower-income households. Some pawn brokers report that higher-value items, including expensive watches, are appearing more frequently as collateral, suggesting the strain could be extending into higher income brackets. Pawn lending typically provides short-term liquidity for borrowers who may not qualify for traditional bank credit, with monthly interest rates ranging from 3% to 25%, which can translate into annualized rates of up to 300%. The industry is often viewed as countercyclical, and recent survey data from an analyst covering FirstCash Holdings Inc. and EZCORP Inc. showed around one-quarter of stores reporting higher activity year over year, compared with 12% seeing a decline, while shares of both companies are trading at five-year highs supported by revenue growth and execution.

At the customer level, early behavioral shifts are becoming more visible. Some borrowers are asking for more time to repay loans, while others are choosing to sell items outright, possibly reflecting concerns about their ability to repay. There are also signs of repeat borrowing cycles, with some customers returning within weeks to pawn the same items again to bridge short-term cash needs. While most operators say they have not yet seen a meaningful increase in defaultspotentially supported by tax refundsthe broader trend suggests financial pressure is building. As one analyst noted, expectations that tax refunds would ease the burden this year have not materialized, with higher gas prices continuing to weigh on consumers.

data.title: Coherent stock jumps 9% as SiC breakthrough powers AI data growth
data.description:
Shares of Coherent Corp moved higher this week after the company unveiled advances in silicon carbide (SiC) epitaxy technology, reinforcing optimism around its role in powering next-generation AI data centers and industrial systems.

The stock rose as much as 9% on April 10 following the announcement, with additional gains supported by broader strength in the optical networking sector.

Coherent shares have also extended a recent rally, climbing more than 16% over four consecutive sessions.

SiC breakthrough targets high-voltage AI applications

Coherent said its latest development enables the production of power devices capable of handling up to 10 kilovolts, a key requirement for high-efficiency energy systems in AI data centers and industrial infrastructure.

The company’s 150 mm and 200 mm thick epitaxy platforms support device architectures up to 10kV in production, with demonstrated capability extending beyond that threshold.

The innovation is aimed at improving performance in applications such as renewable energy, rail systems, fast-charging infrastructure, and grid networks.

“Our new thick epitaxy capability for multi-kilovolt SiC devices enables customers to achieve higher efficiency and power density in critical applications such as energy infrastructure, high-capacity uninterruptible power supplies, and advanced power distribution systems in AI datacenters,” said Gary Ruland, Senior Vice President, Silicon Carbide LLC.

The technology also addresses growing demand from AI data centers, where enhanced power conversion efficiency can lower energy consumption and operating costs.

Sector momentum adds to rally

Coherent’s gains were further supported by positive sentiment across the photonics sector, particularly following strong demand signals from peer Lumentum Holdings Inc..

Lumentum’s CEO indicated the company is on track to sell out its capacity through 2028, reflecting sustained demand from hyperscalers building out AI infrastructure. The company’s shares rose modestly on the news, while Coherent also benefited from the broader momentum.

Both companies have seen substantial gains over the past year, driven by a surge in capital spending by hyperscalers. These firms play a critical role in enabling communication between AI chips, complementing hardware providers such as Nvidia.

Analysts have responded positively to the outlook. TD Cowen analyst Joshua Buchalter raised his price target on Coherent to $340 from $330 while maintaining his rating. Similarly, J.P. Morgan analyst Samik Chatterjee reiterated an Overweight rating and lifted his target to $300 from $245.

Chatterjee noted that investor discussions around the Optical Fiber Communications Conference “collectively delivered a stronger-than-expected level of forward visibility across various growth drivers.”

Valuation and risks remain in focus

Despite the strong momentum, Coherent’s valuation remains elevated. The stock is currently trading at a price-to-earnings ratio of 301.6, reflecting high expectations for future growth.

At the same time, insider activity has pointed to some caution, with approximately $4.9 million in shares sold over the past three months.

Still, the company’s positioning across multiple high-growth markets—including communications, industrial, and energy—continues to support its long-term outlook.

Coherent, which has a market capitalization of about $57.1 billion, is vertically integrated across lasers, transceivers, and optical components.

As demand for efficient, high-voltage power solutions accelerates alongside the expansion of AI data centers, Coherent’s latest SiC advancements could play a pivotal role in shaping its growth trajectory.

data.title: Impactive aims to replace WEX CEO on board in proxy fight, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • Impactive urges investors to elect 3 new directors
  • Signals it is not aware of regulatory issues related to proxy fight
  • Shareholders vote on directors May 5

By Svea Herbst-Bayliss

Impactive Capital is urging WEX shareholders to replace the information management services company's long-serving CEO on its board of directors at next month's annual meeting as the activist investor presses on with a board challenge, according to a regulatory filing made on Friday.

The hedge fund also said it cooperated with regulators on issues that might have tripped up its proxy fight, addressing a potentially critical issue the company raised last month. Impactive owns roughly 5% of WEX's shares.

Reuters first reported Impactive's plans.

Impactive wants to replace three sitting directors, including CEO Melissa Smith, who also serves as board chair, the documents show. The hedge fund is not pushing to oust Smith as CEO but rather to replace her on the board and to split the CEO and chair roles, which are combined at many companies.

WEX's board has 12 directors, but three are retiring at the annual meeting. Impactive had planned to nominate four director candidates but is now nominating three, the filing shows.

LAGGING SHARES, CEO PAY IN FOCUS

The hedge fund blames Smith for the company's lagging share price, says her pay is too high and considers the board's governance record poor.

The hedge fund argues WEX shares have trailed primary competitors Corpay and HealthEquity and the S&P Mid-Cap Index since Smith became chair in September 2019.

During the same period, WEX's market value, now $5.5 billion, has fallen in half, losing shareholders $3.4 billion in value, the hedge fund said. WEX's share price has climbed 28% in the last 12 months.

WEX is primarily focused on fleet management, corporate payments, and employee benefits. Its board and management team have considered Impactive's input on strategy, capital allocation, and board composition, Reuters previously reported.

A WEX representative did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

WEX owns and operates WEX Bank, a Utah-based industrial-chartered bank, and is therefore regulated by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and the Utah Department of Financial Institutions (UDFI).

Impactive also wants to remove WEX director Stephen Smith, who is not related to the CEO and who the fund says was partly responsible for rising management compensation, and Nancy Altobello, who it blames for allowing the CEO to keep her role as board chair even amid underperformance.

Instead, the firm is urging shareholders to elect technology and payments executive Kurt Adams, veteran financial services executive Ellen Alemany and Lauren Taylor Wolfe, one of Impactive's co-founders.

Impactive has spent months preparing for what is shaping up to be one of the year's most closely watched and bitter board fights, which is expected to be decided at the May 5 annual meeting. The hedge fund noted investors had already soured on the CEO last year when Smith received only 64.3% of support from shareholders, dropping from 97.7% support received in 2024.

WEX last month indicated in a regulatory filing there may be trouble ahead for Impactive related to requirements from the FDIC and UDFI for anyone planning to press ahead with a proxy fight.

The hedge fund said it has responded to the regulators' inquiries and received no additional requests from either.

data.title: Commvault explores sale after takeover interest, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • Commvault working with Goldman Sachs after takeover interest, sources say
  • Thoma Bravo among firms expressing takeover interest, sources say
  • Commvault shares shed roughly 60% of value since September

By Milana Vinn

Data protection software provider Commvault Systems is exploring a sale after receiving takeover interest from multiple parties, according to four people familiar with the matter.

Commvault, which has a market capitalization of about $3.5 billion, is working with Goldman Sachs on its options, according to the sources, after fielding inquiries from both private equity firms and strategic buyers.before paring some of the gains, after Reuters reported the

Thoma Bravo is among the potential buyers that have expressed interest in Commvault in recent weeks, said two of the sources. One source said the buyout firm had already made an earlier offer for the company, without specifying when and at what price.

Shares of Commvault jumped more than 17% on Friday, before paring some of the gains, after Reuters reported the company is exploring a sale.

Commvault, Goldman Sachs, and Thoma Bravo declined to comment.

Tinton Falls, New Jersey-based Commvault provides software that helps businesses protect and recover data in the event of cyberattacks, ransomware, system failures or accidental deletion across major enterprise platforms and cloud providers.

Its customers include 3M , Sony and Hilton , according to its website.

Valuations of publicly traded software companies have broadly struggled in recent months, as investors fret over how artificial intelligence is disrupting their business models. After hitting an all-time high on September 18, Commvault shares have subsequently dropped around 60%.

Despite its share performance, Commvault reported strong growth in its most recent quarter, with revenue rising 19% to a record $314 million. Annualized recurring revenue reached $1.085 billion, up 22% on the prior year.

While steep share price drops often catalyze takeover interest from private equity firms, most have skirted new deals for software companies as the AI disruption trade plays out.

Thoma Bravo Managing Partner Orlando Bravo has been open to those, however, saying the software selloff is a “huge buying opportunity.”

Data recovery has emerged as a resilient pocket within the cybersecurity sector, as AI further increases the need for backup and recovery systems instead of displacing them.

data.title: Lido DAO to Host Community Call on on April 23rd
data.description:
Lido DAO will hold Lido Poolside: Institutional on April 23rd to discuss the latest developments in institutional Ethereum staking. Guests will include representatives from Credora, FalconX, Fireblocks, and more.

Refer to the official tweet by LDO:

Lido
@LidoFinance

Lido Poolside: Institutional

Join Lido contributors on Thursday, April 23rd to discuss the latest institutional Ethereum staking developments.

Featuring guests from @CredoraNetwork, @FalconXGlobal, @FireblocksHQ & more.

More info here: https://t.co/HEcOkOHPyl pic.twitter.com/NnFtIJuh9W

Apr 10, 2026

LDO Info

The Lido project is a liquid staking platform for PoS (Proof-of-Stake) cryptocurrencies, which enables users to stake their tokens on Lido and receive tokenized versions of their staked assets at a 1:1 ratio. Lido supports Ethereum 2.0 staking and other Layer 1 PoS blockchains, such as Solana, Polygon, Polkadot, and Kusama. By issuing these tokenized assets, users maintain liquidity and are able to participate in DeFi activities for additional yield while earning staking rewards.

The mechanism of operation for Lido revolves around its unique liquid staking solution. Users can stake any amount of PoS assets, addressing the issues of illiquidity, complexity, and centralization present in traditional PoS staking. When a user deposits assets to Lido, they’re staked on the relevant blockchain via Lido’s smart contract, which manages deposits, withdrawals, fund delegation, fees, token minting and burning, and more. Users receive a tokenized version of their deposited funds (stAsset tokens), which can be used in DeFi protocols and dApps to earn additional rewards.

Regarding the LDO token, it’s important to note that LDO is the native governance token of the Lido protocol. Holders of LDO tokens have the power to vote on proposals in the Lido DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization). The DAO governs the protocol and makes crucial decisions regarding its operation, ensuring alignment with stakeholder interests. The DAO manages fee parameters and distribution, addition and removal of node operators, and other aspects of the protocol, maintaining transparency and decentralization.

data.title: The U.S. and Iran Will Meet This Weekend. What to Expect. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Emily Russell

U.S. and Iranian officials will meet face-to-face this weekend for the first time since the Iran war began six weeks ago.

Vice President JD Vance will represent the U.S. at negotiations in Islamabad on Saturday. Special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, who represented the U.S. in negotiations over Iran's nuclear capabilities before the war, will join Vance in Pakistan's capital. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and Secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council Mohammad Baqir Zulqadr will represent Iran, alongside other Iranian leaders.

Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif will host the talks. Sharif invited the delegations to Islamabad after initiating the two-week cease-fire Tuesday. Pakistan, which has a large Shia Muslim population like Iran, also shares a border and economic ties to the country.

"Right now it looks like Iran has the upper hand in the talks," says Antoine Halff, chief analyst at Kayrros, a climate and energy data analytics company. "They have established if not control of the Strait of Hormuz, at least the ability to detour and reduce traffic dramatically."

Iran's ambassador to Pakistan, Reza Amiri Moghadam, said on X Wednesday that Iran was coming to Islamabad for "serious talks based on the 10 points proposed by Iran." Those 1o points include demands that the U.S. lift all sanctions, permit uranium enrichment on Iranian soil, and allow Iran to continue controlling the strait.

Trump called the 10-point plan a "workable basis on which to negotiate" in his cease-fire announcement on X Tuesday. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt later disputed that the 10-point plan Trump called "workable" is the same 10-point plan Iran is publicly demanding.

"They put forward a more reasonable and entirely different and condensed plan to the president," Leavitt said in a press briefing Wednesday. "The president's red lines, namely the end of uranium enrichment in Iran, haven't changed."

The administration hasn't clarified what terms were included in the plan Iran presented on Tuesday. Trump maintains he is unwavering on his 15-point plan, which calls for Iran to open the strait, stop uranium enrichment, and never develop nuclear weapons.

"It is very unlikely in the next two weeks we are going to have a resolution to all the issues on the table," says Ben Cahill, a nonresident senior fellow and energy expert at the Atlantic Council. "There is just a chasm between the U.S. and Iran."

Cahill says investors will be paying close attention to how negotiators discuss the strait, through which 20% of the world's oil and gas typically flows daily. Iran is still limiting passage through the strait to just a few ships a day and is charging $2 million tolls — despite Trump's cease-fire demand that the strait be fully reopened.

"Any indication that there is some kind of resolution and that Iran is backing off their maximalist demands would be welcome by the oil market, " Cahill says.

Brent crude dropped below $100 a barrel for the first time since the war began after Trump announced the cease-fire. It began to tick back up later in the week as the deal appeared to falter.

"The market is probably underpricing" oil futures, Halff says. "Even if there is an agreement and everything goes well, it is going to take some time to resupply the market and repair the damage."

Trump suggested to ABC News on Wednesday that he is open to reopening the strait through a joint venture, in which Iran and the U.S. share control of and profits from the waterway. Trump had previously said the strait must be opened without tolls.

"The joint venture is something that was proposed by the president. But he wants to see the strait reopened immediately without limitations," Leavitt said Wednesday.

Vance will be limited in the negotiations by international and U.S. laws. Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, for instance, no one country can control waterways it does not exclusively own. And only Congress can approve the removal of sanctions on Iran.

"We anticipate significant congressional pushback to any White House effort to vacate the sanctions," Helima Croft, RBC Capital Markets' head of global commodity strategy, wrote in an analyst note. "Importantly for energy markets, the continuing sanctions status quo would make it exceedingly difficult for shipping companies to pay the sanctioned IRGC transit fees."

Regardless of how discussions about control of the strait go this weekend, the cease-fire talks will be complicated by a party notably absent from Islamabad: Israel.

Israel claims it isn't part of the cease-fire agreement and ramped up attacks against the Iran-backed militant group Hezbollah in neighboring Lebanon this week, killing hundreds in the country's capital, Beirut.

The speaker of Iran's parliament, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, who will be attending the negotiations, wrote on X Thursday that Lebanon is an "inseparable part of the cease-fire." Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Thursday he won't stop attacks on Lebanon, but that he told his cabinet to begin direct talks with Lebanon "as soon as possible."

Reports Friday indicate the two sides will meet in Washington next week. How their discussions evolve could impact the negotiations happening thousands of miles away in Islamabad.

Write to emily.russell@barrons.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
